text: I got one of those Capcom 2.7 super caps and charged it a few times and used it with a flashlight . Wish they weren't so expensive I've got alot of ideas to try with them .

author: @endeshaw1000
text: I can tell its working. I already have figured out an own prototype-cell (10x10x0.3 cm now) which holds a bit more than 250mA. I even was able to produce conduktive ink, with self made graphene (elekro-chem), which I could use to make a 250mA supercapacitor as well but Im not sure if my color is really waterproof. When I try the amount of gum arabic like in the video my conductivity is ultra bad (10 times worse), but with a bit less its working. I still riddling why is that :) ... AND im VERY sure now that i have no more salts in it , but after weeks of home chemistry every day after work i need a break now :)

author: @mauricecotton7885
text:
Hi can anyone help me with a problem I need to make a custom potentiometer for a project that needs a linear resistance of 50k ohms and I need to paint it onto a curved plastic part (ABS)  and the Max size  the resistance strip can be is 1mm +/- 0.5mm thick 2.5mm +/-0.5mm wide and the 50k ohm +/-10% is 15mm long+/-1mm .
I have been trying to use fine graphite powder and binders of different types that can bond to ABS plastic and at a ratio that gives me a 50k ohm at 15mm.
 My brain is fried with this problem 🤪🤯
Any helpful advice would be appreciated.
thanks😁

author: @IvanVoras

author: @alistairwarburton628
text:
I live off grid with solar and an LPG generator. Lead acid is a pain as absorbing simply isn't cost effective and thus battery degradation is a huge problem. Not looking for a handout but would be up for building a huge bank of A4 capacitors, and probably will whatever your thoughts, but wondered if you would be interested in a large scale real world test.
I think that using small boost converters, in banks with up control, will maximize usable capacity in the same way a jule thief does.
I expect that MPPT, to maximise the PV  by limiting charge current, will also be required. It would also be interesting to see how much we can optimise charging from the generator by measuring fuel flow and  applying MPPT to that as well given that the store charge rate will no longer be a limiting factor. Thoughts?
Thanks for all the great content by the way, truly inspiring.
Just as an aside... Would dissimmlar solvents form a mesoporous film if the polymer was only soluble in one, in the same way as the Dot4 you suggested?
How would one test that? conductivity when weted with salt solution perhaps?

author: @totalrewindacademy4877
text:
I realize that with what you are doing there are a thousand different bunny-trails to follow.

That being said, have you seen the recent youtube hubbub over the material "starlite"?  It's a "forgotten" super-material that is more or less a carbon foam that acts as a super-insulator.  Since carbonized materials are kind of your forte I was wondering what you thought?

More Details:

The channel nighthawk kind of kicked off the buzz when he made a pita-bread sized patty out of it capable of protecting his hand from the heat of a blowtorch: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aqR4_UoBIzY

And, like many youtube "secrets"  it could be mere slight-of-hand, but the destructive beyond the press channel tested it against thermite and it actually worked! https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Qtm_xOtw_r0

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I do know about starlite mate - i don't know if you remember we made a carbon foam a few years agon and put it on a lit gas ring with an ice cube on top - so the theory is sound and so i think starlite is real here's the link to the foam video https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ktksuJPwjpo

author: @TibswarsSpartan
text:
On your video "FWG Supercapacitor Finished", you said you're not absolute sure about the capacity, however you made a mistake first and said your whole device got 18V at 1000F instead 18V 100F or 1.8V at 1000F each individual capacitor.
A mistake by multiple factor of 10 is way to much to just say it's just an unprecise estimation, and you didn't yet correct it in a highlighted/pinned comment or in the description.
It was hard to find your correction as answer comment, where you mentioned it once since someone asked the right question.
The critics were because of this quite reasonable, they don't know that you made a mistake.

I'm really interested if your shown individual capacitors come close to 1000F at 1.8V
I currently use 12 maxwell caps, 6 in series 2 in parrallel, which is calculated about 116 Farads in parrallel to the car battery,  it makes my 1.9L diesel car start way more smoothly in the winter.
Your flat capacitors could fit much easier into my car in parallell to my battery, 9 in series would, (expecting them to actually come close to 1000 Farads), make 111F and comes close to my current setup.
8 in series with 125F would exceed my current setup, but runs highly on the voltage limit at 14.4 V which is about the car maximum charging- and spike voltage.

I have a single maxwell cap 350F at 2.7V sitting at home, I didn't fully charge it the last time.
It is probably sitting for one or many more months at ~2.10V. They can actually hold 2.4V very very long aswell.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I agree - i never really thought it through - i will add it to the description - thanks for pointing it out - something else to point out mate 1000f at 2.7v has more energy stored than 1000F at 1.8v - I really like to work in coulombs and joules as it is far more unambiguous but not a lot of people can go with that so i much more often use mWh as I think it means more - Maxwell say 80% retention in 30 days - it seems you are saying pretty much the same thing - cheers

author: @StefanHartmann-hartiberlin
text: Hi Rob , you need to condition your supercaps first with a pulse charger... Maybe best done with a RE-EMF charger... If they are slightly overcharged in the first few charging cycles, they will better keep their voltages, which I experienced with my 500 Farad caps...So we really have to have really good chargers for them and also especially designed DC2DC Stepup converters, that always put out 12 Volts DC, while the supercaps are discharged down to almost zero Volts...This will make sure to really drain all the remaining energy out of them. This will be a good job for the electronic wizzards out there to build great circuits for these batteries. Looking forward to buy a few samples myself. Cheers. Regards, Stefan.

author: @ericlinz4626
text:
Those Chinese supercapacitors are really cheap.  It's not a a surprise they aren't very good.

People use them to build DIY spot welders or other fun hobby projects. 

Good to hear yours are up to the Maxwell level of performance.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Yeah - they are good for that for sure - but not much else I think and cheers mate

replies.author: @bridgerectifier7711
replies.text:
Eric Linz - Yeah, I bought a few of these too, thinking they might be good.
Have you ever shorted them out?  They make some disturbing internal noises, cracking and clicking.  I've found that they are no good for currents above 10 Amps for more than 2 seconds, otherwise their internal discharge rates become progressively worse and worse.
The only application I've found them useful for is to back up my quality 500W 24v sine-wave inverter, otherwise, they are pure shit.
